title: Machine Learning-Based Psychology: A Study to Understand Cognitive Decision-Making
summary: Applied machine learning algorithms to a custom 3-section questionnaire on cognitive decision-making, mapping respondents across youth (15–20), adult (20–24), and millennial (25+) age groups into distinct behavioral clusters.
findings: 5 behavioral clusters identified. Largest: 41.04% 'merciful-silent' profile (forgiven, peace-seeking). Cluster 3 shows 31.21% prioritizing family — a measurable COVID-era shift in values. Demonstrated ML can classify psychological states with high granularity.

title: Machine Learning for Prediction of Nutritional Psychology, Fast Food Consumption and Its Impact on Students
sampleSize: 150+ MIT-WPU School of Engineering students, age 19–22
summary: Multi-model ML study analyzing the relationship between fast food consumption, BMI, and sickness in college students. Five prediction targets tested. Pre-COVID vs Post-COVID eating behavior tracked explicitly.
findings: Decision Tree on sickness frequency = 98% accuracy. Random Forest on BMI = 96.53%. Post-COVID healthy eating jumped from 50% to 83%+. Hostel students spent avg ₹9,000/month on food; metro hostel students ₹10,500/month.

title: Analytical Study on Consumer Social and Behavioral Psychology and Its Influence on Online Purchasing
publisher: CRC Press — Internet of Behaviors (IoB) Book Chapter
summary: Final synthesis applying B.F. Skinner's CBT to digital consumer behavior. 30-question survey across India mapping how social media platform usage, time online, and age group influence online purchasing decisions under the Internet of Behaviors framework.
findings: Adults (20–40) drove 72.36% of all orders. Instagram led platform influence at 75% average. Prolonged social media use directly drives purchases — >2 hours = 45.08% buy-influenced vs 38.53% for <1 hour. November (Diwali) = 13.64% peak purchase month.
